title: TPU v1 · Logical Equivalence Checking: Prove Gate Netlist ≡ RTL after Every Synthesis or ECO
oneLine: Synthesis, optimization, and ECO can silently alter function through bad constraints, scripts, or tool defects, while simulation samples only finite cases. LEC exhaustively compares Boolean cones between golden RTL and the implementation netlist. Equivalence releases the design; failure produces a counterexample showing where and under which inputs behavior diverges. This is the mandatory functional backstop from verified RTL to 28 nm gates.

legend.name: Golden RTL—functional reference
legend.desc: The verified synthesizable RTL from ⑥ describes the 256×256 MAC array, Unified Buffer, accumulators, and CISC control. LEC does not prove that RTL specification correct; it proves that the implementation has not deviated from this chosen golden reference.

legend.name: Implementation netlist—proof target
legend.desc: The 28 nm standard-cell netlist after synthesis, ECO, PPA recovery, or DFT insertion has undergone extensive automated and manual transformation. LEC proves that these implementation changes preserve RTL behavior.

legend.name: LEC engine (Formality / Conformal)
legend.desc: The tool matches corresponding registers, primary I/O, and black boxes between designs, then cuts combinational cones at each endpoint and uses BDD/SAT methods to prove their Boolean functions identical. It is the signoff gate for preserved function.

legend.name: Logical equivalence—exhaustive decision
legend.desc: For every matched endpoint, the two Boolean functions must agree for all possible inputs. Unlike finite simulation sampling, formal proof admits no input that distinguishes them. Overall equivalence requires every endpoint to pass.

legend.name: Non-equivalent → counterexample
legend.desc: If one endpoint differs, LEC produces a concrete input assignment that yields different outputs. Engineers reproduce the discrepancy, identify the optimization, ECO, or constraint error, fix it, and rerun until fully equivalent.

steps.cap: ① Load both sides: verified RTL from ⑥ as golden and the post-synthesis/ECO netlist as implementation. Prove implementation equals reference rather than revalidating the reference.
② Match registers, primary I/O, and black boxes. Incomplete or incorrect matching causes misleading results and must be resolved before proof.
③ At every register input and primary output, cut a logic cone and formally prove both Boolean functions equal for all inputs—not sampled simulation.
④ Equivalent ✓: every endpoint matches, showing synthesis changed only implementation and the gate netlist may pass signoff.
⑤ Non-equivalent ✗: use the counterexample to identify the exact endpoint and input causing divergence, repair the offending optimization/ECO/constraint, and rerun.

before: Choose golden and implementation deliberately: Golden is the verified RTL from ⑥; implementation is the synthesized or ECO netlist. LEC proves implementation ≡ reference, not that the reference specification is correct. An unfrozen golden invalidates a clean result.

Provide libraries and definitions for both sides: Link functional models for 28 nm standard cells, and model SRAM macros such as the Unified Buffer/accumulators consistently as black boxes or equivalent abstractions.

Define matching rules and name mapping: Synthesis may rename, merge, duplicate, or retime registers. Supply naming maps and retiming or phase-inversion settings so corresponding state points match correctly.
Set a hard exit criterion: Require all endpoints equivalent, zero unexplained unmatched or unverified points, and auditable treatment of every exception. Merely running LEC is not acceptance.

Disclosure boundary: Formality/Conformal, matching, cone proof, and counterexamples are standard signoff practice. TPU LEC scripts and results are unpublished; its public structure only illustrates the proof target.
during: Run LEC after every synthesis, optimization, or ECO. Initial synthesis, area/power recovery, scan and clock-gate insertion, and manual ECO all modify the netlist and may alter function. LEC is the standard mathematical backstop after each change.

Clean matching before judging proof. Resolve every unmatched register or endpoint. Legal transformations such as retiming, constant propagation, and dead-register removal need correct tool settings; results based on bad matching are not credible.

Use exhaustive proof, not simulation samples. For each endpoint, convert both cones to Boolean representations and prove equality for all input combinations. Even a fully passing functional simulation cannot cover every synthesis-induced corner.

Debug failure through the counterexample. Reproduce the distinguishing assignment and identify whether a bad false path, retiming setup, ECO connection, or tool issue caused it. Fix the root cause instead of weakening LEC.

Audit manually forced equivalence. Tools allow set-equivalent or disabled points, but every use requires rationale and evidence. Abuse converts real divergence into a false pass, the most dangerous LEC failure mode.

State the boundary honestly: The methodology is general EDA signoff. TPU structure is the equivalence target, but no proprietary scripts, settings, or results are claimed; command examples are illustrative.

result.label: Reference / implementation (example)
result.value: golden = RTL from ⑥; impl = post-synthesis/ECO netlist; prove impl ≡ golden

result.label: Comparison granularity
result.value: Each register input and primary output; exhaustive Boolean-cone proof

result.label: Pass criterion
result.value: All endpoints Equivalent; zero unexplained Unmatched/unverified points; no suspect waivers

result.label: Coverage nature
result.value: Exhaustive across the input space, not sampled simulation

after: Read matching before the verdict: Confirm zero unexplained unmatched endpoints, then verify every comparison is Equivalent. A conclusion built on incomplete matching is invalid.
Review every manual exception: List all set-equivalent or disabled points and justify each. Exceptions are where false passes hide and must be scrutinized at signoff.

Root-cause non-equivalence and iterate: Use the counterexample to distinguish bad constraints, retiming/optimization setup, ECO wiring, or a tool defect. Repair the actual source and rerun until all failures disappear.

Make LEC a synthesis exit gate: Full equivalence is mandatory alongside clean WNS/TNS and acceptable area/power before DFT insertion. Rerun against the appropriate baseline after DFT or backend ECO changes.

Archive evidence for DFT and backend: Deliver LEC reports, matching data, and exception lists with the netlist and SDC. Maintain a “change once, prove once” record through scan reorder and every ECO to tapeout.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the 256×256 8-bit MAC array, 24 MiB Unified Buffer, CISC control, 700 MHz/~92 TOPS, weight-stationary dataflow, and 28 nm/331 mm² are published context. LEC, Formality/Conformal, matching, cone proof, counterexamples, and exit criteria are standard illustrative EDA signoff practice, not private TPU v1 scripts or results.)
